content.text: The original Transformer introduced by Vaswani et al. (2017) paired an encoder stack — which builds bidirectional representations of an entire input sequence — with a decoder stack that generates output tokens one at a time using cross-attention to consult those representations. This encoder–decoder architecture shaped translation, summarization, and many sequence-to-sequence tasks. Modern agent LLMs are nearly always decoder-only, but understanding how the two-part design works — and why it was later replaced for open-ended generation — anchors all transformer-based model reasoning.
An encoder–decoder transformer consists of two distinct stacks of layers sharing the same token-embedding space. The

processes the source sequence — a sentence to translate, a document to summarize, an instruction to follow — using full bidirectional self-attention, so every token can attend to every other token. The output is a set of context vectors, one per source token, called the encoder memory or encoder hidden states.

generates the target sequence one token at a time. Each decoder layer runs three sub-layers in order: masked self-attention over the tokens already generated (preventing the model from looking ahead), cross-attention over the encoder memory (so each generated token can consult the full source), and a feed-forward network. The final decoder layer projects to vocabulary logits for next-token prediction.

Cross-attention is the defining mechanism: queries come from the decoder's running hidden state, while keys and values come from the encoder memory. This asymmetry means the decoder effectively asks the encoder: 'given what I've generated so far, which source tokens are most relevant?' A separate set of K and V projections per layer maps the fixed encoder memory for each cross-attention head.

Encoder–decoder models dominated neural machine translation (Google NMT, 2016) before transformers, and the original Transformer paper validated the design for seq2seq tasks without recurrent networks. T5 (Google, 2019) and BART (Facebook, 2019) demonstrated that an encoder–decoder could be pre-trained on corrupted text and then fine-tuned to near-state-of-the-art on summarization, translation, question answering, and classification.

For agent developers, the relevance is threefold. First, understanding cross-attention clarifies how retrieval-augmented generation patterns work in decoder-only models — when a RAG system prefixes retrieved docs before a query, it simulates a crude form of encoding within the causal context window. Second, some components in production agentic pipelines are still encoder–decoder models: T5 rerankers, BART summarizers, mT5 multilingual translators. Third, multimodal models (Flamingo, LLaVA) use cross-attention between a vision encoder and a language decoder — the same pattern.
— encoder memory is computed once per source; decoder can attend to it on every generation step without re-processing the entire input at each token
— no causal mask in the encoder means every source token can see the full context, producing richer representations than causal prefill in decoder-only models

— for fixed, long source documents (code review, summarization), the encoder compresses them into memory; the decoder generates short, structured output without re-attending the full input at every step
— T5 and BART checkpoints achieve high accuracy on translation, summarization, and slot-filling tasks with moderate compute
— multimodal models reuse decoder cross-attention to inject vision, audio, or retrieval signals without altering generation logic
— encoder and decoder each hold parameters; doubling the serving footprint versus a decoder-only model of similar capacity
Harder to scale for general generation
— the decoder-only design proved more amenable to scaling laws in open-ended generation; GPT and LLaMA families overtook T5 for chat and code
— the encoder reads the source once; if the source changes mid-generation, the encoder must re-run; this complicates multi-turn streaming
No shared context between source and generation
— in decoder-only models the prompt and completion share causal attention; in encoder–decoder they communicate only through cross-attention, limiting certain types of in-context learning
— KV caches must be managed separately for self-attention and cross-attention layers in the decoder

The encoder takes a tokenized source sequence of length N and runs it through L encoder layers. Each layer applies pre-layer-norm, multi-head self-attention across all N positions (no causal mask), a residual connection, another pre-norm, and a feed-forward sublayer. The final output is a matrix of shape [N, d_model] — one vector per source token. These vectors are the encoder memory.

Encoder layer (pre-norm variant):
  h = LayerNorm(x)
  x = x + SelfAttn(h, h, h)   # Q=K=V=h, full bidirectional
  h = LayerNorm(x)
  x = x + FFN(h)
  → output: encoder_memory ∈ ℝ^{N × d_model}

The decoder generates tokens autoregressively. At each generation step, the decoder processes the tokens generated so far (length T) through L decoder layers. Each decoder layer runs three sub-layers: masked self-attention over T positions (causal), cross-attention where Q comes from the decoder hidden states and K, V come from the encoder memory, and a feed-forward sublayer. The final output is projected to vocabulary logits, and the highest-probability (or sampled) token ID is selected.

Decoder layer (pre-norm variant):
  h  = LayerNorm(x)
  x  = x + MaskedSelfAttn(h, h, h)           # causal; Q=K=V=h
  h  = LayerNorm(x)
  x  = x + CrossAttn(Q=h, K=enc_mem, V=enc_mem)  # consult encoder
  h  = LayerNorm(x)
  x  = x + FFN(h)
  → logits = Linear(x[-1])  → softmax → next token
Cross-attention projects decoder hidden states through a query weight matrix (
) and encoder memory through separate key and value weight matrices (

). The attention score for decoder position t attending to encoder position i is softmax(Q_t · K_i / sqrt(d_k)), and the weighted sum of V_i values updates x_t. This lets each generated token dynamically select which source tokens to focus on at that generation step.

attrs.alt: Encoder-decoder vs decoder-only transformer architecture comparison with attention patterns

content.text: The diagram contrasts the two dominant transformer architectures. In the encoder–decoder design, a bidirectional encoder stack produces memory K,V vectors that flow into each decoder layer via cross-attention. The decoder applies a causal mask to its own self-attention while attending freely to the full encoder output. In the decoder-only design, one causal stack processes the entire prompt plus completion; RAG is simulated by placing retrieved text early in the context window so it receives the most causal attention from subsequent tokens.

Encoder–decoder models are best when the source is fixed and long and the target is a compact transformation of it. They are not the right architecture for open-ended chat or multi-turn agent loops where the boundary between 'input' and 'output' shifts each turn.
— T5, mT5, Helsinki-NLP OPUS models; input = source sentence, output = translated sentence
— BART, Pegasus; input = long document, output = short abstract
— slot-filling, form completion, code comment generation where the source is stable
— image captioning (vision encoder → text decoder) or audio transcription (Whisper: audio encoder → text decoder)
for open-ended chat, multi-turn agentic conversations, or tasks where the 'encoder' input changes every turn — decoder-only is better for those

Most production encoder–decoder usage goes through Hugging Face Transformers. The key difference from decoder-only usage is that generation is conditioned on encoder outputs, not just the causal context.

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSeq2SeqLM
import torch

# Load a T5 model for summarization
model_id = "google/flan-t5-base"
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
model = AutoModelForSeq2SeqLM.from_pretrained(model_id, torch_dtype=torch.float16)

# The source text (encoder input)
source = (
    "Byte-Pair Encoding is a subword tokenization algorithm that starts with "
    "individual characters and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs "
    "into new vocabulary entries until reaching the target vocabulary size."
)

# Tokenize the source
inputs = tokenizer(
    f"summarize: {source}",
    return_tensors="pt",
    max_length=512,
    truncation=True,
)

# Generate: encoder runs once, decoder generates autoregressively
with torch.no_grad():
    output_ids = model.generate(
        **inputs,
        max_new_tokens=60,
        num_beams=4,
        early_stopping=True,
    )

summary = tokenizer.decode(output_ids[0], skip_special_tokens=True)
print("Summary:", summary)
For translation, the same pattern applies with a translation-trained model:

from transformers import MarianMTModel, MarianTokenizer

model_name = "Helsinki-NLP/opus-mt-en-fr"
tokenizer = MarianTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = MarianMTModel.from_pretrained(model_name)

sentences = ["The agent called the search tool.", "Results returned in 250ms."]
inputs = tokenizer(sentences, return_tensors="pt", padding=True, truncation=True)

translated = model.generate(**inputs, num_beams=4)
decoded = tokenizer.batch_decode(translated, skip_special_tokens=True)
print(decoded)
The following example shows how to run a BART encoder–decoder for document summarization and inspect the intermediate encoder memory:

from transformers import BartTokenizer, BartForConditionalGeneration
import torch

tokenizer = BartTokenizer.from_pretrained("facebook/bart-large-cnn")
model = BartForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained("facebook/bart-large-cnn")

document = (
    "Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) combines a retriever that searches a "
    "knowledge base with a generator that synthesizes answers. The retriever returns "
    "relevant passages; the generator conditions on both the passages and the query. "
    "This reduces hallucination compared to a generator operating purely from "
    "parametric knowledge, and allows knowledge updates without retraining."
)

inputs = tokenizer(document, return_tensors="pt", max_length=1024, truncation=True)

# Inspect encoder output shape
with torch.no_grad():
    encoder_outputs = model.model.encoder(**inputs)
    encoder_memory = encoder_outputs.last_hidden_state  # [1, N, 1024]
    print("Encoder memory shape:", encoder_memory.shape)

    # Generate summary using the encoder memory
    summary_ids = model.generate(
        inputs["input_ids"],
        max_length=130,
        min_length=30,
        length_penalty=2.0,
        num_beams=4,
    )

print(tokenizer.decode(summary_ids[0], skip_special_tokens=True))

A customer support platform processes 50,000 support tickets per day. Each ticket is several hundred words; agents need a one-sentence summary to route it to the right team. A fine-tuned BART encoder–decoder model runs on GPU with batching:

from transformers import pipeline
from typing import List

# Load a fine-tuned BART for support ticket summarization
summarizer = pipeline(
    "summarization",
    model="models/support-ticket-bart-fine-tuned",
    device=0,
    torch_dtype="float16",
)

def batch_summarize(tickets: List[str], batch_size: int = 32) -> List[str]:
    summaries = []
    for i in range(0, len(tickets), batch_size):
        batch = tickets[i : i + batch_size]
        results = summarizer(
            batch,
            max_length=50,
            min_length=10,
            truncation=True,
        )
        summaries.extend([r["summary_text"] for r in results])
    return summaries

# Typical batch: 100ms per 32 tickets on A100
tickets = [open(f"ticket_{i}.txt").read() for i in range(1000)]
summaries = batch_summarize(tickets)

The encoder–decoder architecture is ideal here: tickets are fixed input, summaries are short structured output, and the encoder can compress a 500-word ticket into memory that the decoder uses to generate a 10-word routing summary. A decoder-only model would need the full ticket in context for every output token, increasing prefill cost unnecessarily.
— open-ended multi-turn conversation does not have a clean source/target boundary; decoder-only models with a chat template are the right choice
— encoder memory should be computed once and reused across beam search or multiple decode passes; re-running the encoder per token wastes compute
— using a decoder-only tokenizer to count source tokens for a T5 model; T5 uses SentencePiece with a different vocabulary
— T5-style models use a specific token to seed the decoder; omitting it causes generation failures or off-by-one errors
— a common copy-paste bug when adapting decoder-only inference code to seq2seq models

# Wrong: using input_ids as decoder_input_ids
output = model.generate(input_ids=enc_ids, decoder_input_ids=enc_ids)  # BUG

# Right: let generate() handle decoder seeding automatically
output = model.generate(input_ids=enc_ids)  # correct
across multiple decode attempts (beam search, sampling) —
in Hugging Face's generate API handles this
— run the encoder on multiple source sequences simultaneously; it is O(N²) in sequence length but parallelizes well on GPU with padding + attention masking
for quality-critical seq2seq tasks; beam search degrades gracefully as beams increase, unlike greedy which can stall on repeated tokens
during fine-tuning — high entropy means the decoder ignores encoder memory; low entropy can indicate copying without reasoning
from test summarizations — encoder–decoder models memorize source patterns aggressively; data leakage inflates eval numbers

Encoder inference runs once per source sequence and scales as O(N²) in self-attention cost. For sources under 512 tokens, modern hardware makes this negligible. Beyond 2K source tokens, encoder inference time grows quadratically; long-document models like LongT5 or LED introduce sparse or local attention patterns to address this.

Decoder inference is the throughput bottleneck at serving time. Cross-attention KV caches for encoder memory must be held in GPU memory alongside self-attention KV caches. For a BART-large decoder (12 layers, 1024 hidden, 16 heads), cross-attention KV caches add roughly 0.5 GB per active sequence. Batching is limited by this memory. Distillation (DistilBART) reduces decoder depth and improves throughput significantly for latency-sensitive applications.
— T5-small through T5-11B, Flan-T5 (instruction-tuned), mT5 (multilingual); checkpoints on Hugging Face Hub
— Facebook's denoising encoder–decoder; excellent for summarization (CNN/DailyMail) and translation (mBART-50)
— 1000+ language-pair translation models built on Marian NMT (encoder–decoder); available on Hugging Face Hub
— OpenAI's encoder–decoder for speech transcription; audio spectrogram encoder → text token decoder; available via Hugging Face and openai-whisper package

# Checking cross-attention patterns (useful for debugging fine-tuning)
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

output = model(
    input_ids=inputs["input_ids"],
    decoder_input_ids=decoder_ids,
    output_attentions=True,
    return_dict=True,
)

# Cross-attention: [batch, heads, decoder_len, encoder_len]
cross_attn = output.cross_attentions[-1][0].mean(0).detach().numpy()
plt.imshow(cross_attn, aspect="auto", cmap="Blues")
plt.xlabel("Encoder token position")
plt.ylabel("Decoder token position")
plt.title("Cross-attention heatmap (last decoder layer, averaged heads)")
plt.colorbar()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("cross_attn.png")

Encoder–decoder models surface the same prompt-injection risks as decoder-only models — a source document crafted to contain instruction-like text can influence decoder behavior. For summarization pipelines that process untrusted documents (emails, web pages), apply length limits on source tokens, sanitize HTML/markdown, and post-process outputs to remove potential injected instructions before passing them downstream.

Cross-attention leakage is a known privacy risk in seq2seq fine-tuning: memorized source sequences from training can surface in generated summaries. Differential privacy training, output filtering, and membership inference audits are mitigation strategies for deployments handling sensitive source documents (medical notes, legal filings).
Think of the encoder as a

. The reader processes the entire source in one pass and compiles a set of notes (encoder memory). The writer then composes the output one word at a time, glancing at those notes via cross-attention whenever it needs to reference the source. A decoder-only model combines reading and writing into one continuous stream — it re-reads its own output as context each step, with the source mixed into that stream via the prompt.
Encoder–decoder models process a fixed source with bidirectional self-attention and generate output via cross-attention to the resulting encoder memory
Cross-attention lets each generated token dynamically query the full source representation — the core mechanism borrowed by multimodal models
Decoder-only LLMs dominate agent APIs; encoder–decoder models remain relevant for seq2seq tasks, translation, and multimodal architectures
Two KV caches must be managed at serving time: one for decoder self-attention and one for encoder cross-attention
T5 and BART are the most commonly fine-tuned encoder–decoder checkpoints; Whisper uses the architecture for audio
Simulate encoder–decoder in decoder-only RAG by placing source context early in the prompt where causal attention is most complete
Never use encoder–decoder for open-ended multi-turn conversations — decoder-only with a chat template is the correct pattern
